coredata.dc:title: Green synthesis of High-Yield red fluorescent carbon dots from Amaranth for sensitive water detection in organic solvents
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Ma, Linping
Liao, Shihua
Liu, Jiawen
Ma, Yanshun
coredata.dc:description: With the increasing demand for eco-friendly materials, the green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from natural sources has gained significant attention for applications in sensing and imaging. This study introduces a novel solvothermal method to synthesize high-yield red fluorescent CDs (R-CDs) from Amaranth, a sustainable and low-cost vegetable. The resulting R-CDs exhibit a remarkable quantum yield of 42.0 %, surpassing the performance of CDs derived from other natural sources. Characterization revealed a graphene core with surface functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, and –NH2. The R-CDs display excitation wavelength-independent emission at 601 nm and excellent stability under various environmental conditions. They were applied as a probe for detecting trace water in organic solvents, demonstrating high sensitivity, a broad linear detection range, and a low detection limit of approximately 5 ppm, attributed to the low solubility of CDs in water. This work underscores the potential of R-CDs as stable and efficient optical materials for environmental sensing, particularly in detecting water content in organic solvents, with broader implications for quality control in chemical and fuel industries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Amaranth
Carbon Dots
Solvothermal Method
Quantum Yield

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Application: Probe for detecting water content in organic solvents

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Structural characterization of R-CDs (a) TEM image (b) particle size distribution (c) X-ray diffraction pattern; (d) Raman spectroscopy; (e) Infrared spectroscopy; (f) XPS survey spectrum; high-resolution XPS spectra of (g-i) C1s, N1s and O1s.

Optical Properties of R-CDs (a) The excitation-emission matrix; (b) Ultraviolet–visible absorption, fluorescence excitation and emission spectra; (c) Emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths; (d) Normalized emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths.

The influences of various environmental factors on the luminescence properties of R-CDs. (a) Photobleaching resistance, (b) Salt resistance, (c) Temperature stability, (d) Temporal stability. (e) PL emission spectra and (f) Quantum yields of R-CDs in various solvents.

R-CDs serving as ethanol concentration detector in wine. (a) The change of fluorescence intensity of R-CDs in different concentrations of ethanol solution; (b) Linear fitting of R-CDs fluorescence intensity and water content in ethanol solution; (c) UV–Vis spectrum of R-CDs in Water and Ethanol; (d)Fluorescence decay traces of the probe and probe + Water.
Detection limit of R-CDs for water content in different solvents.
Comparison with previously reported probes in the literature.

head.ce:title: Green synthesis of High-Yield red fluorescent carbon dots from Amaranth for sensitive water detection in organic solvents

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: With the increasing demand for eco-friendly materials, the green synthesis of carbon dots (CDs) from natural sources has gained significant attention for applications in sensing and imaging. This study introduces a novel solvothermal method to synthesize high-yield red fluorescent CDs (R-CDs) from Amaranth, a sustainable and low-cost vegetable. The resulting R-CDs exhibit a remarkable quantum yield of 42.0 %, surpassing the performance of CDs derived from other natural sources. Characterization revealed a graphene core with surface functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, and –NH2. The R-CDs display excitation wavelength-independent emission at 601 nm and excellent stability under various environmental conditions. They were applied as a probe for detecting trace water in organic solvents, demonstrating high sensitivity, a broad linear detection range, and a low detection limit of approximately 5 ppm, attributed to the low solubility of CDs in water. This work underscores the potential of R-CDs as stable and efficient optical materials for environmental sensing, particularly in detecting water content in organic solvents, with broader implications for quality control in chemical and fuel industries.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent times, as environmental consciousness has gained prominence, an increasing number of researchers have redirected their efforts towards exploring carbon dots (CDs) derived from natural sources [1]. Compared to synthetic carbon sources used for carbon dot preparation, leveraging natural products offers several advantages: Firstly, these resources are renewable, abundantly available, and cost-effective [2,3]. Secondly, most natural products contain heteroatoms, which facilitate the synthesis of heteroatom-doped CDs without the need for additional chemical reagents containing heteroatoms, thereby reducing environmental pollution [4,5]. Lastly, natural products exhibit good biocompatibility, broadening the potential applications of the resulting CDs [6–8]. Consequently, employing natural products presents a green and straightforward approach to CD preparation. However, the quantum yield of CDs synthesized from natural products reported in literature is generally low, and the emission wavelengths are concentrated in the short-wave region. Furthermore, the presence of impurities and by-products adversely affects the luminescent performance and quality of fluorescent CDs, hindering their practical application and promotion. The limited understanding of the reaction mechanisms also restricts the application of CDs derived from natural products [9,10]. Therefore, developing an efficient and convenient strategy for the preparation of CDs from natural products holds significant practical value.

In the bottom-up synthesis approach, one-step hydrothermal methods offer simplicity, mild reaction conditions, low-cost precursors, and environmental friendliness [11–13]. Utilizing biomass obtained from nature as a precursor has several benefits, such as the renewability of natural products, low pollution levels, and widespread distribution, which fundamentally aligns with environmental protection. Based on these studies, many scholars prefer hydrothermal methods using natural products as precursors for the synthesis of CDs, especially employing biomass waste as precursors, which embodies the concept of 'turning waste into treasure', adhering to the principles of 'green chemistry' [14,15].

The presence of trace amounts of water in organic solvents can significantly impact water-sensitive reactions [16,17]. Water in fuels can also pose serious problems. For instance, trace moisture in aviation gasoline may condense into ice, blocking the fuel system and threatening human safety [18]. Therefore, rapid and sensitive detection of trace water in organic solvents is crucial. Although technologies such as titration and chromatography can be used to analyze trace water [19,20], these methods require expensive instruments and complex experimental operations. In contrast, simple and convenient fluorescent detection is more favored.

CDs synthesized from o-phenylenediamine as a precursor often exhibit solvent-dependent fluorescence [21,22], which is related to their nitrogen-containing chromophores and benzene ring structures. Moreover, the ease of surface modification of CDs during synthesis, which readily introduces electron-withdrawing groups (such as C = O) onto the CD surface, ultimately forms an A-π-D structure consisting of electron acceptors, π-conjugated domains, and electron donors, leading to an intramolecular charge transfer effect (ICT) [23–25]. This makes the fluorescence of CDs sensitive to environmental polarity, resulting in solvent-dependent luminescence [26–28]. Such CDs show great potential in the detection of trace water in organic solvents.

Based on the aforementioned research, the preparation of carbon dot fluorescent probes with high sensitivity to water, broad applicability, and high selectivity is of great significance. In this work, we utilized Amaranth, a common, sustainable, and low-cost vegetable, as the carbon source and o-phenylenediamine as the nitrogen source to synthesize red fluorescent CDs (R-CDs) through a solvothermal method. To remove impurities from the CDs, we employed a chromatographic column method adapted from organic synthesis to purify the product solution, resulting in R-CDs with a remarkable quantum yield of 42.0 %. We characterized the structure, surface composition, and optical properties of the synthesized R-CDs, and investigated their quantum yield in different solvents, demonstrating their excellent salt tolerance and photostability. We further explored the water content in various organic solvents, achieving rapid and convenient detection of water content in organic solvents. Additionally, we investigated the possible mechanism of red carbon dot synthesis, which is crucial for the development of high-performance fluorescent probes with longer wavelengths.

Amaranth was purchased from a local supermarket in Chengdu. o-Phenylenediamine (OPD, analytical grade) and quinine sulfate (analytical grade) were acquired from Shanghai Aladdin Bio-technology Co., Ltd. Nitric acid and ethanol (analytical grade) were obtained from Chengdu Kelon Chemicals Co., Ltd. The hydrothermal reaction vessel (25 mL) was procured from Xiniu Technology Co., Ltd. The ultrasonic cleaner was purchased from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd. The electric heating constant temperature convection drying oven was acquired from Shanghai Lichen Technology Co., Ltd. UV–vis spectra were measured using a Shenyang Hengping 756 PC UV–visible spectrophotometer, and fluorescence spectra were obtained using a Lingguang F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

Amaranth was utilized as the primary precursor, and ethanol was selected as the solvent for the synthesis of R-CDs via the solvothermal method. The detailed procedure is as follows: 10 g of amaranth was blended with 200 mL of ethanol in a stirring apparatus and allowed to soak for 12 h. 5 mL of the filtrate was mixed with 0.6 g of o-phenylenediamine and 0.5 g of nitric acid, followed by the addition of another 5 mL of ethanol, and the mixture was sonicated for 10 min and then transferred to a high-pressure vessel lined with polytetrafluoroethylene (PTFE) and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling naturally to room temperature, the product solution was first subjected to centrifugal separation to remove large particulate impurities, followed by purification using column chromatography, resulting in the acquisition of the R-CDs solution. The preparation process is depicted in Fig. 1 . The possible reaction mechanism (Fig. S1) of R-CDs synthesis and control experiment (Fig. S2) are enclosed in the supplementary materials.

The relative fluorescence quantum yield (QY), a key metric for evaluating the luminescence efficiency of fluorescent materials, is defined as the ratio of emitted photons to absorbed photons. In this study, quinine sulfate was used as the reference standard, with a known quantum yield of 0.54 in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution[29]. The reference solution was prepared by dissolving quinine sulfate in 0.1 M sulfuric acid to achieve an absorbance of 0.05 at 360 nm. The fluorescence quantum yield of the samples was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
QY =
F u
F r
×
A r
A u
×
η u 2
η r 2
where, QY denotes the fluorescence quantum yield; F u and F r are the integrated areas under the fluorescence emission spectra of the sample and the reference standard, respectively; A u and A r correspond to the absorbances of the sample and the reference standard, respectively; η u and η r represent the refractive indices of the solvents for the sample and the reference standard, respectively. This equation allows for the accurate determination of the sample's fluorescence quantum yield by normalizing for differences in absorbance, refractive index, and the fluorescence intensity of the sample compared to the standard.

The structure and chemical composition of R-CDs were investigated using various analytical techniques, including Transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Dynamic light scattering (DLS), X-ray diffraction (XRD), Raman spectroscopy (RS), Fourier-transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR), and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The morphology of the R-CDs was examined via TEM, as shown in Fig. 2 a, revealing that the particles are nearly spherical but exhibit some size irregularities. The particle size distribution of the R-CDs was measured using DLS technology, with an average diameter of 4.6 nm, as indicated in Fig. 2b. The crystallinity and graphite structure of the R-CDs were further analyzed through XRD patterns. Fig. 2c depicts a broad peak at 25.4°, corresponding to the (002) plane of graphitic carbon[30], which may arise from the small size and controlled carbonization of the R-CDs, also reflecting their amorphous nature. The Raman spectrum of the R-CDs (Fig. 2d) shows two peaks at 1542 cm−1 and 1384 cm−1, associated with the G band and D band of graphene, respectively. The intensity ratio of the D band to the G band is 1.0, indicating a significant presence of defects within the R-CDs[31]. FT-IR was employed to characterize the surface functional groups of the R-CDs. As depicted in Fig. 2e, the peak at 3500 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of O-H/N-H, while the peak at 1550 cm−1 represents the C = O stretching vibrations. These results suggest the presence of functional groups such as –NH2 and −C = O, as well as possibly –OH, confirming the successful incorporation of nitrogen into the R-CDs. To further investigate the surface composition of the R-CDs, XPS analysis was conducted. Fig. 2f shows the survey scan spectrum with characteristic peaks at 533.2 eV, 399.8 eV, and 286.4 eV, corresponding to the O 1 s, N 1 s, and C 1 s regions, respectively. This indicates that the CDs are primarily composed of carbon (C), nitrogen (N), and oxygen (O), with atomic ratios of 78.36 %, 16.84 %, and 4.8 %, respectively. Deconvolution of the C 1 s peak yielded three distinct peaks at 285.8 eV, 284.7 eV, and 286.3 eV (Fig. 2g), which are associated with the functional groups C-N, C-C/C = C, and C-O. The deconvolution of the N 1 s peak revealed peaks at 399.1 eV, 400.5 eV, and 401.4 eV (Fig. 2h), indicating that nitrogen is predominantly present as pyridinic N and amino N. The O 1 s peak at 532.6 eV (Fig. 2i) is attributed to the C-O bond. In conjunction with the IR results, it can be inferred that the R-CDs possess a graphene core and that their surface may contain functional groups such as –OH, –COOH, and –NH2.

The fundamental photoluminescent properties of R-CDs were investigated using ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy, excitation spectroscopy, and emission spectroscopy. The three-dimensional excitation and emission spectra of R-CDs are depicted in Fig. 3 (a), where R-CDs exhibit relatively complex excitation and emission peaks. Fig. 3(b) illustrates the UV–Vis absorption spectrum along with the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum shows a strong absorption band below 400 nm, characteristic of the π → π* electronic transition of aromatic benzene rings derived from sp2 hybridized carbon atoms. The absorption and excitation peaks closely aligned near 500 to 600 nm represent the characteristic absorption peaks of R-CDs, primarily attributed to the electronic transitions of surface functional groups on the CDs. These absorbed photons participate in the fluorescence emission process, suggesting that these functional groups may be components of the luminescent centers of the CDs. R-CDs possess multiple excitation peaks, with the maximum excitation peak at 562 nm and the maximum emission wavelength at 601 nm. By color-coding the emission spectrum according to wavelength, it can be observed from the figure that the synthesized CDs are predominantly red-emitting CDs. Furthermore, the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the R-CD emission spectrum is 75 nm.

To investigate the photoluminescent behavior of R-CDs under different excitation wavelengths, the emission spectra of R-CDs were measured at excitation wavelengths ranging from 525 to 575 nm. The results are presented in Fig. 3(c). It is evident from the figure that the morphology and intensity of the emission spectra of R-CDs exhibit certain variations with different excitation wavelengths. When these emission spectra are normalized, as shown in Fig. 3(d), it becomes clear that the emission peaks do not shift significantly. This phenomenon indicates that the emission wavelength of R-CDs is independent of the excitation wavelength. It suggests that the luminescence of R-CDs arises from fixed electronic transition states rather than extensive adjustments of the excited state energy levels. Typically, this luminescent characteristic is attributed to specific inherent defects or structures within the CDs. These properties make R-CDs particularly suitable for applications requiring stable light sources, such as optical imaging, sensing, and bio-labeling, as their luminescent characteristics remain consistent regardless of variations in excitation wavelength.

The stability of CDs is crucial for practical applications. In this study, we investigated the effects of various environmental factors on the luminescent performance of R-CDs, including UV exposure time, NaCl concentration, temperature, storage duration, and different solvents. The resistance of R-CDs to photobleaching was preliminarily assessed by monitoring changes in fluorescence intensity under UV irradiation. After 2 h of UV exposure, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs remained relatively unchanged, as shown in Fig. 4 (a), indicating excellent photostability. Next, we examined the salt tolerance of R-CDs by studying the impact of different concentrations of NaCl solutions on their fluorescence intensity. As depicted in Fig. 4(b), there were no significant changes in the fluorescence peak position or intensity as the NaCl concentration increased from 0 mM to 500 mM. This suggests that NaCl has minimal effect on the luminescent properties of b-CDs, G-CDs, and R-CDs; even in high-concentration NaCl environments, the CDs can maintain their luminescent performance. To investigate the thermal stability of R-CDs, fluorescence intensity measurements were conducted within a temperature range of 30 °C to 60 °C. As shown in Fig. 4(c), the fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs solution exhibited only slight variations with increasing temperature, indicating excellent thermal stability, which supports their application in high-temperature environments. The temporal stability results are presented in Fig. 4(d). After storage at 4 °C for 7 days, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs showed only a negligible decrease. These experimental observations demonstrate that the CDs derived from beetroot exhibit outstanding stability, providing strong support for their subsequent applications.

The solvent environment has a significant impact on the luminescent properties of CDs. In this study, several commonly used laboratory solvents were selected for analysis: water (H2O), ethanol (EtOH), acetone (AC), tetrahydrofuran (THF), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF). The R-CDs were added to each solvent at the same concentration, and their fluorescence emission spectra were measured, as shown in Fig. 4(e). It was observed that R-CDs exhibited the highest fluorescence intensity in acetone, while the lowest intensity was recorded in water. To further investigate the influence of different solvents on the luminescent performance of CDs, the quantum yields of R-CDs in various solvents were calculated using quinine sulfate as a reference standard, as indicated in Equation (1) and presented in Fig. 4(f). The quantum yields of R-CDs in different solvents were found to be as follows: water: 0 %, acetonitrile: 40.6 %, ethanol: 25.3 %, acetone: 42.0 %, THF: 36.6 %, and DMF: 32.9 %. The results demonstrate a variation in the quantum yields of CDs across different solvents, with nearly zero fluorescence intensity in water and the highest quantum yield of 42.0 % observed in acetone. The R-CDs synthesized in this study exhibit high luminescent capability, surpassing the luminescence efficiency of many other CDs. Furthermore, the photoluminescent properties of CDs are related to the polarity of the solvent; selecting an appropriate solvent can further enhance the luminescence efficiency of CDs, providing broader development opportunities for their applications.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application: Probe for detecting water content in organic solvents
ce:section.ce:para: The effect of water content in organic solvents is detailed in the supplementary materials (Fig. S3), demonstrating the high sensitivity of R-CDs to water, a valuable characteristic for applications in water detection during organic synthesis and quality control of organic solvents. To quantitatively investigate this behavior, ethanol solutions with varying water contents (0 %–100 %) were prepared. Equal amounts of R-CDs solution were added to these mixtures, stirred for homogeneity, and equilibrated for 20 min before spectral measurements. As shown in Fig. 5 a, fluorescence intensity of the R-CDs decreases linearly with increasing water content. The linear fitting equation,1-I/I 0 = 1.0649C(H2O)-0.10484, exhibits a strong correlation coefficient (R 2 = 0.9957), indicating high accuracy for quantifying water content. This highlights the probe's potential for analyzing alcohol content in beverages. The detection limit (LOD) was calculated using LOD = 3σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation of blank solution fluorescence intensity (based on 10 measurements), and K is the slope of the calibration curve. Table 1 summarizes the LODs of R-CDs for various organic solvents. Notably, the detection limit for acetone is approximately 12 ppm, while lower limits are observed in other solvents: around 6 ppm for DMF, methanol, and ethanol, and about 3 ppm for THF and DMSO. These results demonstrate the excellent sensitivity and accuracy of R-CDs as a fluorescence probe for water detection.

Furthermore, we compared R-CDs with other fluorescent probes used for detecting water content in organic solvents, as shown in Table 2 . This comparison reveals that R-CDs not only have advantages in the linear detection range but also in terms of LOD. Therefore, they can be effectively utilized for accurately detecting water content in real samples.

To investigate the detection mechanism, we measured the absorbance of R-CDs dissolved in water and ethanol. As shown in Fig. 5c, the comparison of the two curves revealed no new absorption peaks, and there was no significant overlap below 250 nm. However, we measured the fluorescence lifetime of the R-CDs to be 6.34 ns, which decreased to 1.83 ns after adding water, as illustrated in Fig. 5d. We hypothesize that as the water content in the organic solvent–water mixture increases, the original solvent components are replaced by water. Due to the high polarity of water, the overall polarity of the mixed solution increases with the water content. Consequently, the solubility of the CDs decreases, leading to aggregation of the CDs, which in turn results in fluorescence quenching[36].

In this study, red fluorescent CDs (R-CDs) with excellent optical properties were synthesized using the natural product amaranth as a precursor and ethanol as a solvent via a solvothermal method. The R-CDs feature nitrogen- and oxygen-enriched surface functional groups and exhibit excitation wavelength-independent emissions at 601 nm. They demonstrate remarkable photobleaching resistance, salt tolerance, and chemical stability, with a high quantum yield of up to 42.0 % in acetone. As water content increases, hydrogen-bonded organic compounds on the R-CDs are replaced by water, increasing polarity and causing significant fluorescence quenching in aqueous solutions. This property enables the detection of water in organic solvents. For ethanol solutions with water content ranging from 0 % to 100 %, the fluorescence intensity of R-CDs showed a strong linear correlation (R 2 = 0.9957), with a detection limit as low as 5 ppm. The quenching mechanism was attributed to reduced solubility of R-CDs in water, leading to fluorescence quenching. Compared to other probes, this method offers a lower detection limit and simpler operation, making it suitable for applications such as analyzing alcohol content in beverages. The study highlights the potential of R-CDs as a stable and efficient optical material for detecting water content in organic solvents. Additionally, the use of natural products as precursors and the high quantum yield of the R-CDs contribute to their environmental sustainability and practical utility.

Linping Ma: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation. Shihua Liao: Writing – original draft, Validation, Methodology, Investigation. Jiawen Liu: Writing – original draft, Validation, Investigation. Yanshun Ma: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Thank Prof. Dongmao Zhang, Ph.D Joanna Xiuzhu Xu, Xue Leng et al. in Department of Chemistry, Mississippi State University for helpful advices.

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.112654.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1
